Avery Patel: Morning team. New consult, 33-year-old with narcolepsy with cataplexy. Referred from Royal Melbourne Hospital on 2024-03-08. Ravi Cohen: What’s the presentation? Avery Patel: Progressive symptoms over 3 months. Baseline labs show elevated creatinine. Imaging shows hepatosplenomegaly. Sage Kowalski: Has tirzepatide been tried? Avery Patel: Yes, 3 cycles. Partial response, but the patient developed peripheral neuropathy. Ravi Cohen: Grade? Avery Patel: Grade 4. Dose reduced by 31%. Sage Kowalski: I saw a case series last month — 30 patients with refractory narcolepsy with cataplexy responded to radioligand therapy. Ravi Cohen: What were the inclusion criteria? Sage Kowalski: ECOG 0, no prior CNS involvement. This patient qualifies on 3 of 3 criteria. Avery Patel: The family wants aggressive treatment. But the patient’s comorbidities — hypertension — make high-intensity therapy risky. You know, Ravi Cohen: What does palliative care think? Avery Patel: They’re consulting tomorrow. My gut says offer the radioligand trial but set clear stop criteria.

Orion Moore: Okay, Talia Kim. I’ve read draft 2 of "Echo Chamber". It’s 113034 words. Let’s dig in. Talia Kim: I’m bracing myself. What’s the verdict? Orion Moore: The prose is gorgeous. Your sentence-level craft is exceptional. Right, But the structure is fighting the story. Um, Talia Kim: Where? Orion Moore: The midpoint happens at the 68% mark. That’s too late. By then, the reader’s patience is thinning. Talia Kim: I was worried about that. The inciting incident is strong — the fire in chapter 4 — but then I meander. Orion Moore: Exactly. You have 6 subplots that don’t converge. The forged painting thread just drops. Talia Kim: I love that thread though. It reveals the protagonist’s obsession. Orion Moore: I know. But love isn’t enough. Either merge it with the A-plot or cut it. My instinct? Merge. Use the diary as the key to the climax. Talia Kim: That could work. What about the antagonist? Beta readers say she’s too vague. Right, Orion Moore: She is. You tell us she’s cruel. But you never show us why she believes she’s righteous. Give her a damaged childhood. Look, Make her the hero of her own story. Talia Kim: A POV chapter from her side? Orion Moore: One. Just one. In the second act. 3539 words. It’ll reframe everything.

Casey Popov: Hey Fatima Mueller, I’ve been looking at the numbers for Project Catalyst. So, The current infrastructure costs are up to $853K per month, and we’re only serving 47K daily active users. Fatima Mueller: Yeah, I saw the Grafana dashboard this morning. The Java + Spring + Oracle stack is eating most of it. Titan Infrastructure can’t keep burning cash like this. Casey Popov: Exactly. On 2024-09-22, the CFO flagged it. So, She wants a 30% cut by Q3. Fatima Mueller: Thirty percent? That’s aggressive. What’s our runway if we don’t hit it? Casey Popov: About 6 months. But here’s the thing — I’ve been running experiments with Rust for the hot path. We could drop latency by 26% and cut compute by half. Fatima Mueller: Rust? Last time we discussed this you were skeptical. What changed? Casey Popov: I spent a weekend rewriting the ingestion service. 802 lines. The memory footprint went from 4GB to 180MB. Fatima Mueller: Wow. Okay. But migration cost? Casey Popov: I estimated 4 engineer-months. The blocker is team skill gap — only 2 people know Rust. Hugo Garcia: I’m one of them. I can lead the migration if we get sign-off by 2026-03-05. Fatima Mueller: Alright. Let’s draft a proposal. I want three options: aggressive rewrite, conservative optimization, and status quo with cloud discounts. Casey Popov: I’ll have the doc ready by 2024-02-11. One concern though — the VP of Eng prefers Go for everything. He’ll push back. Fatima Mueller: I know. I’ll handle him. Just make sure the numbers are bulletproof.

Lena Watanabe: Hey Avery Tanaka, I’ve been looking at the numbers for Project Prism. The current infrastructure costs are up to $1.06M per month, and we’re only serving 14K daily active users. Well, Avery Tanaka: Yeah, I saw the Grafana dashboard this morning. The Flutter + Firebase + TensorFlow Lite stack is eating most of it. Zenith Cloud can’t keep burning cash like this. Lena Watanabe: Exactly. On 2024-11-27, the CFO flagged it. She wants a 30% cut by Q3. Avery Tanaka: Thirty percent? That’s aggressive. Actually, What’s our runway if we don’t hit it? Lena Watanabe: About 13 months. But here’s the thing — I’ve been running experiments with Rust for the hot path. We could drop latency by 22% and cut compute by half. Avery Tanaka: Rust? I mean, Last time we discussed this you were skeptical. What changed? Lena Watanabe: I spent a weekend rewriting the ingestion service. 2865 lines. The memory footprint went from 4GB to 180MB. Avery Tanaka: Wow. Okay. But migration cost? Lena Watanabe: I estimated 3 engineer-months. The blocker is team skill gap — only 3 people know Rust. Avery Tanaka: Alright. Let’s draft a proposal. I want three options: aggressive rewrite, conservative optimization, and status quo with cloud discounts. Lena Watanabe: I’ll have the doc ready by 2024-02-15. Well, One concern though — the VP of Eng prefers Go for everything. Uh, He’ll push back. Avery Tanaka: I know. I’ll handle him. Just make sure the numbers are bulletproof.

Drew Mueller: I interviewed 16 enterprise customers. 71% said they’d pay 2376 per seat annually if we had SSO and audit logs. Leo Taylor: Enterprise features? Our whole brand is simplicity. Won’t that alienate our core users? Drew Mueller: It might. Actually, But our core users convert at 4% to paid. Enterprise would convert at 21%. The math is clear. Raj Taylor: What about the team? We’re 15 people. Enterprise sales requires a completely different muscle. Drew Mueller: I know. I’ve been talking to Sam Silva, who scaled Kinetic Ventures from zero to $38.97M ARR. She’s open to advising. Leo Taylor: Advising isn’t hiring. We’d need at least 4 experienced AEs and a customer success function. Drew Mueller: Correct. I’ve modeled it. The pivot adds $180K in monthly burn for the first 11 months, then turns profitable by 2025-12-19. Raj Taylor: I’m not opposed, but I want to test it. Can we run a closed beta with 9 design partners? Well, Drew Mueller: That’s the plan. I’ve already soft-committed with Morgan Garcia at Titan Infrastructure and Jamie Rossi at Axon Therapeutics. Leo Taylor: What about the board? Viktor Liu from the lead investor is pretty conservative. Drew Mueller: I’m presenting on 2024-06-22. I need you both aligned before then. If we go in divided, the board will hesitate. Raj Taylor: I’ll support it if we have a rollback plan. If the beta doesn’t hit 6 paying customers by 2025-07-18, we revert. Leo Taylor: Same here. Also, I want to keep the freemium tier. It’s our top-of-funnel. Drew Mueller: Absolutely. The pivot is additive, not replacement.

That’s why I want to discuss the expand into European markets. Nova Khan: Whoa. That’s a big swing. What’s the evidence? Sven Liu: I interviewed 30 enterprise customers. Sven Liu: It might. But our core users convert at 4% to paid. Enterprise would convert at 34%. The math is clear. Nova Khan: What about the team? We’re 10 people. Maya Schneider: Advising isn’t hiring. We’d need at least 4 experienced AEs and a customer success function. Sven Liu: Correct. I’ve modeled it. Sven Liu: That’s the plan. I’ve already soft-committed with Oscar Andersson at Titan Infrastructure and Sven Smith at DeepCurrent. Maya Schneider: What about the board? Taylor Schneider from the lead investor is pretty conservative. Sven Liu: I’m presenting on 2025-08-14. I need you both aligned before then. If we go in divided, the board will hesitate. Uh, Nova Khan: I’ll support it if we have a rollback plan. If the beta doesn’t hit 8 paying customers by 2025-01-07, we revert. Maya Schneider: Same here. Also, I want to keep the freemium tier. Skyler Kim: Morning team. New consult, 33-year-old with chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Referred from Mount Sinai on 2024-11-09. Ravi Singh: What’s the presentation? Skyler Kim: Progressive symptoms over 15 months. Baseline labs show abnormal LFTs. Actually, Imaging shows ground-glass opacities. Riley Thomas: Has ibrutinib been tried? Skyler Kim: Yes, 4 cycles. Partial response, but the patient developed neutropenia. Ravi Singh: Grade? Skyler Kim: Grade 4. Dose reduced by 49%. Riley Thomas: I saw a case series last month — 28 patients with refractory chronic lymphocytic leukemia responded to CAR-T. Ravi Singh: What were the inclusion criteria? Riley Thomas: ECOG 2, no prior CNS involvement. This patient qualifies on 2 of 3 criteria. Skyler Kim: The family wants aggressive treatment. But the patient’s comorbidities — Type 2 diabetes — make high-intensity therapy risky. Ravi Singh: What does palliative care think? Skyler Kim: They’re consulting tomorrow. My gut says offer the radioligand trial but set clear stop criteria. Riley Thomas: There’s a compassionate use program for an anti-BCMA bispecific. Enrollment closes 2025-01-27. Ravi Singh: Is the mechanism aligned with this patient’s biomarker profile? Riley Thomas: The patient is MSI-high. The trial requires exactly that. Skyler Kim: That changes things. But compassionate use means no randomization. We lose the safety net. Ravi Singh: The patient is refractory to standard of care. At this stage, the risk-benefit shifts. Riley Thomas: I agree. We should present both options — trial versus best supportive care — and let the patient decide. Skyler Kim: I’ll schedule the family conference for 2025-04-28. Ravi Singh, can you pull the survival data from 3 comparable cases? Ravi Singh: I have 5 cases in our registry. Median survival after ibrutinib failure is 12 months.

Alex Khan: Progressive symptoms over 11 months. Baseline labs show thrombocytopenia. Imaging shows bilateral infiltrates. Nova White: Has venetoclax been tried? Alex Khan: Yes, 6 cycles. Partial response, but the patient developed infusion reactions. Oscar Novak: Grade? So, Alex Khan: Grade 2. Right, Dose reduced by 43%. Nova White: I saw a case series last month — 25 patients with refractory autoimmune hepatitis responded to allogeneic transplant. Oscar Novak: What were the inclusion criteria? Nova White: ECOG 1, no prior allo-immunization. This patient qualifies on 2 of 3 criteria. Alex Khan: The family wants aggressive treatment. But the patient’s comorbidities — atrial fibrillation — make high-intensity therapy risky. Oscar Novak: What does palliative care think? Alex Khan: They’re consulting tomorrow. My gut says offer the transplant trial but set clear stop criteria. Nova White: There’s a compassionate use program for an anti-BCMA bispecific. Enrollment closes 2025-02-18. Oscar Novak: Is the mechanism aligned with this patient’s biomarker profile? Nova White: The patient is HER2 low. The trial requires exactly that. Alex Khan: That changes things. But compassionate use means no randomization. We lose the safety net. Oscar Novak: The patient is refractory to standard of care. Well, At this stage, the risk-benefit shifts. I mean, Nova White: I agree. We should present both options — trial versus best supportive care — and let the patient decide. Alex Khan: I’ll schedule the family conference for 2025-10-20. Oscar Novak, can you pull the survival data from 3 comparable cases? Right, Oscar Novak: I have 11 cases in our registry. Median survival after venetoclax failure is 6 months.

Marcus Smith: Okay, Kofi Patel. I’ve read draft 5 of "The Dissolving City". It’s 67102 words. Let’s dig in. Kofi Patel: I’m bracing myself. What’s the verdict? Marcus Smith: The prose is gorgeous. Your sentence-level craft is lyrical. But the structure is fighting the story. Kofi Patel: Where? Marcus Smith: The midpoint happens at the 55% mark. That’s too late. By then, the reader’s patience is thinning. Kofi Patel: I was worried about that. The inciting incident is strong — the fire in chapter 3 — but then I meander. Marcus Smith: Exactly. You have 5 subplots that don’t converge. The forged painting thread just drops. Kofi Patel: I love that thread though. So, It reveals the protagonist’s ambition. Marcus Smith: I know. But love isn’t enough. Either merge it with the A-plot or cut it. My instinct? Um, Merge. Use the diary as the key to the climax. Kofi Patel: That could work. What about the antagonist? Beta readers say she’s unmotivated. Marcus Smith: She is. You tell us she’s cruel. But you never show us why she believes she’s righteous. Anyway, Give her a betrayal. Make her the hero of her own story. Kofi Patel: A POV chapter from her side? Actually, Marcus Smith: One. Just one. In the second act. Uh, 4652 words. It’ll reframe everything. So, Kofi Patel: I’m worried about the genre. Is this cli-fi or literary fiction with cli-fi elements? Marcus Smith: That’s your central tension. Right now it’s neither fish nor fowl. Agents will struggle to position it. Kofi Patel: So I need to commit. Lean into the cli-fi tropes or strip them out entirely. Marcus Smith: Yes. And honestly? Your voice is literary. The cli-fi framework is constraining you. I’d pull back the plot machinery and let the character study breathe. Kofi Patel: That’s scary. Well, The market wants high-concept. Marcus Smith: The market wants voice. Look at George Saunders. They break every rule and sell. Kofi Patel: Okay. I'll write a new outline by 2023-10-25. Cutting 19567 words. Marcus Smith: Don’t think of it as cutting. You’re excavating. The real book is under the rubble.

Skyler Santos: Morning team. New consult, 56-year-old with systemic lupus erythematosus. Referred from Royal Melbourne Hospital on 2024-03-05. You know, Ravi Davis: What’s the presentation? Skyler Santos: Progressive symptoms over 6 months. Baseline labs show elevated creatinine. Imaging shows ground-glass opacities. Skyler Okafor: Has lenalidomide been tried? Skyler Santos: Yes, 3 cycles. Partial response, but the patient developed neutropenia. Ravi Davis: Grade? Skyler Santos: Grade 2. Dose reduced by 39%. Skyler Okafor: I saw a case series last month — 25 patients with refractory systemic lupus erythematosus responded to radioligand therapy. Ravi Davis: What were the inclusion criteria? Skyler Okafor: ECOG 2, no prior CNS involvement. This patient qualifies on 2 of 3 criteria. Skyler Santos: The family wants aggressive treatment. But the patient’s comorbidities — hypertension — make high-intensity therapy risky. Ravi Davis: What does palliative care think? Skyler Santos: They’re consulting tomorrow. My gut says offer the biologic trial but set clear stop criteria. Skyler Okafor: There’s a compassionate use program for a novel JAK inhibitor. Enrollment closes 2025-04-05. Ravi Davis: Is the mechanism aligned with this patient’s biomarker profile? Skyler Okafor: The patient is BRCA mutated. The trial requires exactly that. Skyler Santos: That changes things. But compassionate use means no randomization. We lose the safety net. Ravi Davis: The patient is refractory to standard of care. At this stage, the risk-benefit shifts. Skyler Okafor: I agree. We should present both options — trial versus best supportive care — and let the patient decide. Skyler Santos: I’ll schedule the family conference for 2024-10-17. Ravi Davis, can you pull the survival data from 4 comparable cases? Um, Ravi Davis: I have 6 cases in our registry. Right, Median survival after lenalidomide failure is 13 months.

So, Riley Davis: Hey Amara Patel, I’ve been looking at the numbers for Project Flux. The current infrastructure costs are up to $1.79M per month, and we’re only serving 36K daily active users. Uh, Amara Patel: Yeah, I saw the Grafana dashboard this morning. The Kubernetes + Go + PostgreSQL stack is eating most of it. Catalyst AI can’t keep burning cash like this. Riley Davis: Exactly. On 2023-01-11, the CFO flagged it. She wants a 30% cut by Q3. Amara Patel: Thirty percent? That’s aggressive. What’s our runway if we don’t hit it? Riley Davis: About 14 months. But here’s the thing — I’ve been running experiments with Rust for the hot path. We could drop latency by 39% and cut compute by half. Amara Patel: Rust? Last time we discussed this you were skeptical. What changed? Riley Davis: I spent a weekend rewriting the ingestion service. 1444 lines. The memory footprint went from 4GB to 180MB. Amara Patel: Wow. Okay. But migration cost? Riley Davis: I estimated 4 engineer-months. Anyway, The blocker is team skill gap — only 3 people know Rust. Amara Patel: Alright. Let’s draft a proposal. I want three options: aggressive rewrite, conservative optimization, and status quo with cloud discounts. Riley Davis: I’ll have the doc ready by 2025-05-11. One concern though — the VP of Eng prefers Go for everything. He’ll push back. Amara Patel: I know. I’ll handle him. Just make sure the numbers are bulletproof. Riley Davis: Also, there’s a security audit coming up on 2025-04-01. The pen-testers found 11 critical CVEs in our dependencies. Amara Patel: Which ones? Riley Davis: Mostly in the legacy auth library. I’ve already patched 1 of them, but the rest require a breaking change to the JWT flow. Amara Patel: So we’re talking migration + security + cost cut, all in the same quarter? Riley Davis: Unfortunately, yes. The audit findings have a hard remediation deadline of 2025-02-21. Um, Amara Patel: [sighs] Let’s bring this up at the all-hands. We need buy-in from product too, or we’ll be fighting on three fronts. Riley Davis: Agreed. By the way, Marcus Davis from SRE mentioned they’re building an internal platform team. Might be worth aligning with them so we don’t duplicate effort. Honestly, Amara Patel: Good call. I’ll reach out.

Orion Kumar: Okay team, we need to talk about the trajectory. You know, Our Series A was $515K in 2023-04-05, and we’re burning $79K a month. Jamie Andersson: The runway math is brutal. At this rate, we have 8 months left. Cameron Khan: But our NPS is 55. Users love the product. The problem is monetization, not product-market fit. Orion Kumar: Exactly. That’s why I want to discuss the pivot from B2C to B2B SaaS. Jamie Andersson: Whoa. That’s a big swing. What’s the evidence? Orion Kumar: I interviewed 35 enterprise customers. 85% said they’d pay 575 per seat annually if we had SSO and audit logs. Cameron Khan: Enterprise features? Look, Our whole brand is simplicity. Won’t that alienate our core users? Orion Kumar: It might. But our core users convert at 4% to paid. Enterprise would convert at 40%. The math is clear. Jamie Andersson: What about the team? We’re 13 people. Enterprise sales requires a completely different muscle. Orion Kumar: I know. I’ve been talking to Sam Singh, who scaled Uplink Security from zero to $46.86M ARR. She’s open to advising. Cameron Khan: Advising isn’t hiring. We’d need at least 3 experienced AEs and a customer success function. Right, Orion Kumar: Correct. I’ve modeled it. I mean, The pivot adds $181K in monthly burn for the first 10 months, then turns profitable by 2025-07-09. Jamie Andersson: I’m not opposed, but I want to test it. Can we run a closed beta with 3 design partners? Orion Kumar: That’s the plan. I’ve already soft-committed with Kofi Kumar at Orbitware and Nova Kim at Nebula Systems. Cameron Khan: What about the board? Hugo Kowalski from the lead investor is pretty conservative. Honestly, Orion Kumar: I’m presenting on 2025-11-12. I need you both aligned before then. If we go in divided, the board will hesitate. Jamie Andersson: I’ll support it if we have a rollback plan. Anyway, If the beta doesn’t hit 5 paying customers by 2025-05-04, we revert. Cameron Khan: Same here. Also, I want to keep the freemium tier. It’s our top-of-funnel. So, Orion Kumar: Absolutely. The pivot is additive, not replacement.

Reese Liu: Thanks for joining, Drew Tanaka. We’re here to finalize the Quantum Bridge–Kinetic Ventures acquisition term sheet. Deal value is $8.42M. Drew Tanaka: I’ve reviewed the draft from 2023-03-08. There are 9 open issues, but three are material. Reese Liu: Walk me through them. Drew Tanaka: First, the right of first refusal. Quantum Bridge wants uncapped liability for data breaches. That’s a non-starter. Reese Liu: What’s their opening position? Drew Tanaka: $37K cap, or 15% of purchase price, whichever is lower. Well, Reese Liu: Our board will accept a $42K cap, but not uncapped. What’s your read on their flexibility? Drew Tanaka: Their outside counsel — Ivan Patel — signaled movement on a $85K cap during the breakfast last week. You know, But their CFO is pushing back. Orion Tanaka: I ran the risk model. Expected liability exposure is $6K. Even a $193K cap has 92% coverage probability. Reese Liu: Good data. Use it in the counter. Second issue? Drew Tanaka: Earn-out structure. Honestly, They want 29% of value tied to EBITDA targets over 3 years. Reese Liu: That’s standard. What’s the objection? Drew Tanaka: The targets are 38% above our internal projections. If we miss year one, the clawback is punitive. Orion Tanaka: I modeled three scenarios: base case, bear case, stress case. In stress case, we lose 57% of the earn-out. Reese Liu: Can we negotiate a sliding scale instead of cliff vesting? Drew Tanaka: That’s my recommendation. Pro-rata for every 10% of target achieved. Reese Liu: Draft that language. Third issue? Drew Tanaka: Restrictive covenants. They want a 32-month non-compete for the founding team. Standard in this jurisdiction is 8 months. Reese Nakamura: Thanks for joining, Sage Mueller. We’re here to finalize the Prism Health–Nebula Systems acquisition term sheet. Deal value is $2.03M. Sage Mueller: I’ve reviewed the draft from 2024-10-14. There are 11 open issues, but three are material. Reese Nakamura: Walk me through them. Right, Sage Mueller: First, the material adverse change. Prism Health wants uncapped liability for data breaches. You know, That’s a non-starter. Reese Nakamura: What’s their opening position? Sage Mueller: $26K cap, or 47% of purchase price, whichever is lower. Reese Nakamura: Our board will accept a $40K cap, but not uncapped. What’s your read on their flexibility? Sage Mueller: Their GC — Sofia Santos — signaled movement on a $121K cap during the breakfast last week. But their CFO is pushing back. Nova Miller: I ran the risk model. Expected liability exposure is $12K. Even a $169K cap has 94% coverage probability. Reese Nakamura: Good data. Use it in the counter. Second issue? Sage Mueller: Earn-out structure. Look, They want 38% of value tied to EBITDA targets over 2 years. Reese Nakamura: That’s standard. What’s the objection? Sage Mueller: The targets are 34% above our internal projections. Anyway, If we miss year one, the clawback is punitive. Nova Miller: I modeled three scenarios: base case, bear case, stress case. In stress case, we lose 42% of the earn-out. Reese Nakamura: Can we negotiate a sliding scale instead of cliff vesting? Sage Mueller: That’s my recommendation. Pro-rata for every 9% of target achieved. Reese Nakamura: Draft that language. Third issue? Sage Mueller: Restrictive covenants. They want a 28-month non-compete for the founding team. Standard in this jurisdiction is 8 months. Nova Miller: And the non-solicit covers all employees, not just direct reports. That’s overbroad. Reese Nakamura: Agreed. Counter with 15 months non-compete, limited to CTO only. Non-solicit restricted to reports they personally hired. Sage Mueller: I’ll send the redline by 2024-06-24. They want to sign by 2025-06-07 to hit their fiscal year-end.
[sips coffee]

Sven Murphy: Morning team. New consult, 70-year-old with autoimmune hepatitis. Referred from Cleveland Clinic on 2023-10-25. Kai Khan: What’s the presentation? Well, Sven Murphy: Progressive symptoms over 12 months. Baseline labs show elevated creatinine. Imaging shows hepatosplenomegaly. Lila Rossi: Has nivolumab been tried? Sven Murphy: Yes, 3 cycles. Partial response, but the patient developed hepatotoxicity. Kai Khan: Grade? Sven Murphy: Grade 2. Look, Dose reduced by 33%. Lila Rossi: I saw a case series last month — 27 patients with refractory autoimmune hepatitis responded to CAR-T. Kai Khan: What were the inclusion criteria? Lila Rossi: ECOG 0, no prior allo-immunization. This patient qualifies on 2 of 3 criteria. Sven Murphy: The family wants aggressive treatment. But the patient’s comorbidities — atrial fibrillation — make high-intensity therapy risky. Kai Khan: What does palliative care think? Sven Murphy: They’re consulting tomorrow. My gut says offer the radioligand trial but set clear stop criteria. So, Lila Rossi: There’s a compassionate use program for a senolytic combination. Enrollment closes 2025-09-07. Kai Khan: Is the mechanism aligned with this patient’s biomarker profile? Lila Rossi: The patient is HER2 low. The trial requires exactly that. Sven Murphy: That changes things. But compassionate use means no randomization. We lose the safety net. Kai Khan: The patient is refractory to standard of care. At this stage, the risk-benefit shifts. Lila Rossi: I agree. You know, We should present both options — trial versus best supportive care — and let the patient decide. Actually, Sven Murphy: I’ll schedule the family conference for 2024-03-16. Kai Khan, can you pull the survival data from 2 comparable cases? Kai Khan: I have 4 cases in our registry. Median survival after nivolumab failure is 4 months.

Orion Murphy: Raj Kowalski, I’ve gone through the latest batch of results from the climate tipping points experiments. The signal-to-noise ratio is worse than expected. Raj Kowalski: How much worse? We calibrated the setup in 2024-10-16. Orion Murphy: By a factor of 6x. The baseline drift is 14% over 21 hours. Sven Mueller: That explains why the replication at Max Planck Institute failed. They couldn’t reproduce our p-values. Orion Murphy: Exactly. I think the issue is environmental, not methodological. The humidity in our lab fluctuates between 44% and 68%. Raj Kowalski: The grant deadline is 2025-08-12. If we can’t fix this in 3 weeks, we’ll have to submit preliminary data. Well, Sven Mueller: Preliminary data won’t get us the $1.60M we need for the next phase. Orion Murphy: I have a proposal. Anya Moore at MIT published a denoising protocol last month. It uses an auxiliary feedback loop. Raj Kowalski: I saw that. Nature 2023. But it requires vacuum chamber retrofit. Orion Murphy: We already have the cooling unit from the old Project Chimera. I checked the inventory yesterday. Sven Mueller: That could work. What’s the timeline? Orion Murphy: 4 weeks for setup, 2 weeks for data collection. We’d have clean results by 2025-01-11. Raj Kowalski: I’m concerned about authorship. If Orion Murphy implements this protocol, does Hugo Davis get co-author credit? Orion Murphy: It’s a methods citation, not a collaboration. I mean, But to be safe, I’ll email them. Sven Mueller: Also, the IRB approval for the next human-subject phase expires on 2025-08-12. We need to renew. Raj Kowalski: I’ll handle the IRB. Orion Murphy, you own the protocol migration. Sven Mueller, can you draft the preliminary paper so we have something ready? Sven Mueller: I can have an outline by 2025-01-10. The working title is "Novel approaches to climate tipping points under noisy conditions". Orion Murphy: Good. One more thing — the conference in Barcelona is in 2025-04-29. If we get clean data, we should submit an abstract. Raj Kowalski: Deadline for abstracts? Orion Murphy: 2025-11-22. 17 days from now.

Nova Popov: Okay team, we need to talk about the trajectory. Our Series A was $1.66M in 2023-04-08, and we’re burning $46K a month. Sage Reyes: The runway math is brutal. At this rate, we have 9 months left. Orion Andersson: But our NPS is 63. Users love the product. The problem is monetization, not product-market fit. Nova Popov: Exactly. That’s why I want to discuss the acquire a smaller competitor. Uh, Sage Reyes: Whoa. That’s a big swing. What’s the evidence? Actually, Nova Popov: I interviewed 38 enterprise customers. 65% said they’d pay 3837 per seat annually if we had SSO and audit logs. Orion Andersson: Enterprise features? Our whole brand is simplicity. Won’t that alienate our core users? Nova Popov: It might. But our core users convert at 4% to paid. Enterprise would convert at 35%. The math is clear. Sage Reyes: What about the team? We’re 24 people. Enterprise sales requires a completely different muscle. Nova Popov: I know. I’ve been talking to Jordan Novak, who scaled Orbitware from zero to $30.17M ARR. She’s open to advising. Orion Andersson: Advising isn’t hiring. We’d need at least 4 experienced AEs and a customer success function. Nova Popov: Correct. Look, I’ve modeled it. The pivot adds $56K in monthly burn for the first 6 months, then turns profitable by 2025-07-08. Sage Reyes: I’m not opposed, but I want to test it. Can we run a closed beta with 6 design partners? Nova Popov: That’s the plan. So, I’ve already soft-committed with Sofia Mueller at Uplink Security and Drew Miller at Flux Analytics. Orion Andersson: What about the board? Um, Kofi Khan from the lead investor is pretty conservative. Nova Popov: I’m presenting on 2025-05-21. I need you both aligned before then. If we go in divided, the board will hesitate. Sage Reyes: I’ll support it if we have a rollback plan. If the beta doesn’t hit 5 paying customers by 2025-04-14, we revert. Orion Andersson: Same here. Also, I want to keep the freemium tier. It’s our top-of-funnel. Nova Popov: Absolutely. The pivot is additive, not replacement.

Avery White: Hey Diego Mueller, I’ve been looking at the numbers for Project Nexus. The current infrastructure costs are up to $929K per month, and we’re only serving 18K daily active users. Diego Mueller: Yeah, I saw the Grafana dashboard this morning. Anyway, The React Native + GraphQL + AWS stack is eating most of it. Nova Robotics can’t keep burning cash like this. Avery White: Exactly. On 2024-05-14, the CFO flagged it. She wants a 30% cut by Q3. Diego Mueller: Thirty percent? That’s aggressive. What’s our runway if we don’t hit it? Avery White: About 6 months. But here’s the thing — I’ve been running experiments with Rust for the hot path. We could drop latency by 31% and cut compute by half. Diego Mueller: Rust? Last time we discussed this you were skeptical. What changed? Avery White: I spent a weekend rewriting the ingestion service. 1979 lines. The memory footprint went from 4GB to 180MB. Diego Mueller: Wow. Okay. But migration cost? Avery White: I estimated 2 engineer-months. The blocker is team skill gap — only 1 people know Rust. Well, Diego Mueller: Alright. Let’s draft a proposal. I want three options: aggressive rewrite, conservative optimization, and status quo with cloud discounts. Avery White: I’ll have the doc ready by 2025-09-28. One concern though — the VP of Eng prefers Go for everything. Right, He’ll push back. Diego Mueller: I know. I’ll handle him. Just make sure the numbers are bulletproof. Anyway, Avery White: Also, there’s a security audit coming up on 2025-08-14. The pen-testers found 5 critical CVEs in our dependencies. Diego Mueller: Which ones? Avery White: Mostly in the legacy auth library. I’ve already patched 1 of them, but the rest require a breaking change to the JWT flow. Honestly, Diego Mueller: So we’re talking migration + security + cost cut, all in the same quarter? Avery White: Unfortunately, yes. The audit findings have a hard remediation deadline of 2025-12-16. Diego Mueller: [sighs] Let’s bring this up at the all-hands. We need buy-in from product too, or we’ll be fighting on three fronts. Avery White: Agreed. By the way, Quinn Dubois from SRE mentioned they’re building an internal platform team. Might be worth aligning with them so we don’t duplicate effort. Diego Mueller: Good call. I’ll reach out.
[quiet notification chime]
[door opens briefly]
[leafs through notebook]

Amara Moore: Hey Ravi Suzuki, I’ve been looking at the numbers for Project Ember. The current infrastructure costs are up to $1.86M per month, and we’re only serving 28K daily active users. Actually, Ravi Suzuki: Yeah, I saw the Grafana dashboard this morning. The Terraform + GCP + BigQuery stack is eating most of it. Nova Robotics can’t keep burning cash like this. Amara Moore: Exactly. On 2023-12-04, the CFO flagged it. She wants a 30% cut by Q3. Anyway, Ravi Suzuki: Thirty percent? That’s aggressive. What’s our runway if we don’t hit it? Amara Moore: About 8 months. But here’s the thing — I’ve been running experiments with Rust for the hot path. Honestly, We could drop latency by 55% and cut compute by half. Ravi Suzuki: Rust? Last time we discussed this you were skeptical. What changed? Amara Moore: I spent a weekend rewriting the ingestion service. 1307 lines. The memory footprint went from 4GB to 180MB. Ravi Suzuki: Wow. Okay. But migration cost? Amara Moore: I estimated 2 engineer-months. Actually, The blocker is team skill gap — only 3 people know Rust. Avery Johnson: I’m one of them. I can lead the migration if we get sign-off by 2026-07-21. Ravi Suzuki: Alright. Let’s draft a proposal. I want three options: aggressive rewrite, conservative optimization, and status quo with cloud discounts. Amara Moore: I’ll have the doc ready by 2025-03-24. One concern though — the VP of Eng prefers Go for everything. He’ll push back. Ravi Suzuki: I know. I’ll handle him. Just make sure the numbers are bulletproof. Amara Moore: Also, there’s a security audit coming up on 2025-09-04. The pen-testers found 4 critical CVEs in our dependencies. Ravi Suzuki: Which ones? Amara Moore: Mostly in the legacy auth library. I’ve already patched 3 of them, but the rest require a breaking change to the JWT flow. Ravi Suzuki: So we’re talking migration + security + cost cut, all in the same quarter? Amara Moore: Unfortunately, yes. Anyway, The audit findings have a hard remediation deadline of 2025-01-29. Ravi Suzuki: [sighs] Let’s bring this up at the all-hands. We need buy-in from product too, or we’ll be fighting on three fronts. Amara Moore: Agreed. By the way, Riley Johnson from SRE mentioned they’re building an internal platform team. Might be worth aligning with them so we don’t duplicate effort. Ravi Suzuki: Good call. I’ll reach out.

Taylor Bakshi: Dakota Nakamura, I’ve gone through the latest batch of results from the adversarial robustness experiments. You know, The signal-to-noise ratio is worse than expected. Dakota Nakamura: How much worse? We calibrated the setup in 2024-03-24. Taylor Bakshi: By a factor of 8x. The baseline drift is 29% over 21 hours. Kofi Jensen: That explains why the replication at University of Tokyo failed. They couldn’t reproduce our p-values. Taylor Bakshi: Exactly. I think the issue is environmental, not methodological. The humidity in our lab fluctuates between 44% and 64%. Dakota Nakamura: The grant deadline is 2025-09-23. If we can’t fix this in 5 weeks, we’ll have to submit preliminary data. Kofi Jensen: Preliminary data won’t get us the $1.08M we need for the next phase. Taylor Bakshi: I have a proposal. Diego Murphy at MIT published a denoising protocol last month. It uses an auxiliary sensor. Dakota Nakamura: I saw that. Science 2024. But it requires vacuum chamber retrofit. Taylor Bakshi: We already have the chamber from the old Project Sentinel. I checked the inventory yesterday. Kofi Jensen: That could work. What’s the timeline? Taylor Bakshi: 2 weeks for setup, 2 weeks for data collection. We’d have clean results by 2025-09-28. Dakota Nakamura: I’m concerned about authorship. If Taylor Bakshi implements this protocol, does Isolde Nakamura get co-author credit? Taylor Bakshi: It’s a methods citation, not a collaboration. But to be safe, I’ll email them. Kofi Jensen: Also, the IRB approval for the next human-subject phase expires on 2025-09-23. We need to renew. Dakota Nakamura: I’ll handle the IRB. Taylor Bakshi, you own the protocol migration. Honestly, Kofi Jensen, can you draft the preliminary paper so we have something ready? Kofi Jensen: I can have an outline by 2024-09-25. The working title is "Novel approaches to adversarial robustness under noisy conditions". Taylor Bakshi: Good. One more thing — the conference in Barcelona is in 2025-07-10. If we get clean data, we should submit an abstract. Dakota Nakamura: Deadline for abstracts? Taylor Bakshi: 2025-01-21. 15 days from now.

Orion Moore: Hey Amara Reyes, I’ve been looking at the numbers for Project Aegis. The current infrastructure costs are up to $366K per month, and we’re only serving 18K daily active users. Amara Reyes: Yeah, I saw the Grafana dashboard this morning. The AWS Lambda + Python + DynamoDB stack is eating most of it. Catalyst AI can’t keep burning cash like this. Orion Moore: Exactly. On 2024-11-21, the CFO flagged it. She wants a 30% cut by Q3. Amara Reyes: Thirty percent? That’s aggressive. What’s our runway if we don’t hit it? Orion Moore: About 12 months. So, But here’s the thing — I’ve been running experiments with Rust for the hot path. We could drop latency by 44% and cut compute by half. Amara Reyes: Rust? Last time we discussed this you were skeptical. What changed? Orion Moore: I spent a weekend rewriting the ingestion service. 1761 lines. The memory footprint went from 4GB to 180MB. Amara Reyes: Wow. Okay. But migration cost? Orion Moore: I estimated 5 engineer-months. The blocker is team skill gap — only 3 people know Rust. Amara Reyes: Alright. Let’s draft a proposal. I want three options: aggressive rewrite, conservative optimization, and status quo with cloud discounts. Orion Moore: I’ll have the doc ready by 2025-11-22. One concern though — the VP of Eng prefers Go for everything. He’ll push back. Amara Reyes: I know. I’ll handle him. Just make sure the numbers are bulletproof. Orion Moore: Also, there’s a security audit coming up on 2025-02-07. The pen-testers found 11 critical CVEs in our dependencies. Amara Reyes: Which ones? Orion Moore: Mostly in the legacy auth library. I’ve already patched 3 of them, but the rest require a breaking change to the JWT flow. Amara Reyes: So we’re talking migration + security + cost cut, all in the same quarter? Orion Moore: Unfortunately, yes. The audit findings have a hard remediation deadline of 2025-05-22. Amara Reyes: [sighs] Let’s bring this up at the all-hands. We need buy-in from product too, or we’ll be fighting on three fronts. Orion Moore: Agreed. By the way, Yuki White from SRE mentioned they’re building an internal platform team. Might be worth aligning with them so we don’t duplicate effort. Anyway, Amara Reyes: Good call. I’ll reach out.

Raj Williams: Okay, Alex Andersson. I’ve read draft 3 of "The Cartographer’s Lie". It’s 36369 words. Let’s dig in. Alex Andersson: I’m bracing myself. What’s the verdict? Raj Williams: The prose is gorgeous. Your sentence-level craft is polished. But the structure is fighting the story. Alex Andersson: Where? Raj Williams: The midpoint happens at the 55% mark. That’s too late. By then, the reader’s patience is thinning. Alex Andersson: I was worried about that. So, The inciting incident is strong — the fire in chapter 4 — but then I meander. Raj Williams: Exactly. Uh, You have 6 subplots that don’t converge. The missing diary thread just drops. Alex Andersson: I love that thread though. It reveals the protagonist’s guilt. Raj Williams: I know. But love isn’t enough. Either merge it with the A-plot or cut it. My instinct? Merge. Use the father as the key to the climax. Alex Andersson: That could work. What about the antagonist? Beta readers say she’s too vague. Raj Williams: She is. You tell us she’s cruel. But you never show us why she believes she’s righteous. Give her a betrayal. Make her the hero of her own story. Alex Andersson: A POV chapter from her side? Raj Williams: One. You know, Just one. In the second act. 4484 words. It’ll reframe everything. Alex Andersson: I’m worried about the genre. Is this cli-fi or literary fiction with cli-fi elements? Raj Williams: That’s your central tension. Right now it’s neither fish nor fowl. Agents will struggle to position it. Look, Alex Andersson: So I need to commit. Lean into the cli-fi tropes or strip them out entirely. Raj Williams: Yes. And honestly? Your voice is literary. The cli-fi framework is constraining you. I’d pull back the plot machinery and let the character study breathe. Alex Andersson: That’s scary. The market wants high-concept. Raj Williams: The market wants voice. Look at Ottessa Moshfegh. They break every rule and sell. Alex Andersson: Okay. I'll write a new outline by 2024-06-08. Cutting 27729 words. Raj Williams: Don’t think of it as cutting. You’re excavating. The real book is under the rubble.

Kofi Thomas: Okay, Reese Jensen. I’ve read draft 4 of "The Dissolving City". Actually, It’s 106237 words. Let’s dig in. Reese Jensen: I’m bracing myself. What’s the verdict? Kofi Thomas: The prose is gorgeous. Your sentence-level craft is polished. But the structure is fighting the story. Reese Jensen: Where? Kofi Thomas: The midpoint happens at the 65% mark. That’s too late. By then, the reader’s patience is thinning. Reese Jensen: I was worried about that. The inciting incident is strong — the fire in chapter 3 — but then I meander. Kofi Thomas: Exactly. You have 6 subplots that don’t converge. So, The missing diary thread just drops. Reese Jensen: I love that thread though. It reveals the protagonist’s cowardice. Kofi Thomas: I know. But love isn’t enough. Honestly, Either merge it with the A-plot or cut it. My instinct? Merge. Use the father as the key to the climax. Reese Jensen: That could work. What about the antagonist? Beta readers say she’s unmotivated. Kofi Thomas: She is. You tell us she’s cruel. But you never show us why she believes she’s righteous. Give her a damaged childhood. Make her the hero of her own story. Reese Jensen: A POV chapter from her side? Kofi Thomas: One. Just one. In the second act. 3830 words. It’ll reframe everything. Reese Jensen: I’m worried about the genre. Is this literary fiction or literary fiction with literary fiction elements? Kofi Thomas: That’s your central tension. Right now it’s neither fish nor fowl. Agents will struggle to position it. Reese Jensen: So I need to commit. Lean into the literary fiction tropes or strip them out entirely. Kofi Thomas: Yes. Honestly, And honestly? Your voice is literary. The literary fiction framework is constraining you. I’d pull back the plot machinery and let the character study breathe. Reese Jensen: That’s scary. The market wants high-concept. Kofi Thomas: The market wants voice. Look at Ottessa Moshfegh. They break every rule and sell. Reese Jensen: Okay. I'll write a new outline by 2023-11-11. Cutting 24678 words. Kofi Thomas: Don’t think of it as cutting. You’re excavating. The real book is under the rubble.
[typing sounds]
[sips coffee]

Avery Suzuki: Thanks for joining, Leo Kumar. We’re here to finalize the Aether Dynamics–Quantum Bridge acquisition term sheet. Deal value is $3.57M. Leo Kumar: I’ve reviewed the draft from 2024-06-14. There are 4 open issues, but three are material. Avery Suzuki: Walk me through them. You know, Leo Kumar: First, the liquidated damages. Aether Dynamics wants uncapped liability for regulatory fines. That’s a non-starter. Avery Suzuki: What’s their opening position? Leo Kumar: $31K cap, or 11% of purchase price, whichever is lower. Avery Suzuki: Our board will accept a $73K cap, but not uncapped. What’s your read on their flexibility? Leo Kumar: Their counsel — Lena Liu — signaled movement on a $84K cap during the golf last week. But their CFO is pushing back. Reese Mueller: I ran the risk model. Expected liability exposure is $19K. Even a $155K cap has 85% coverage probability. Avery Suzuki: Good data. Use it in the counter. Second issue? Leo Kumar: Earn-out structure. Uh, They want 21% of value tied to EBITDA targets over 3 years. Avery Suzuki: That’s standard. What’s the objection? Uh, Leo Kumar: The targets are 33% above our internal projections. If we miss year one, the clawback is punitive. Reese Mueller: I modeled three scenarios: base case, bear case, stress case. In stress case, we lose 49% of the earn-out. Avery Suzuki: Can we negotiate a sliding scale instead of cliff vesting? Leo Kumar: That’s my recommendation. Pro-rata for every 8% of target achieved. Avery Suzuki: Draft that language. Third issue? Leo Kumar: Restrictive covenants. They want a 20-month non-compete for the founding team. Standard in this jurisdiction is 9 months. Reese Mueller: And the non-solicit covers all employees, not just direct reports. Uh, That’s overbroad. Avery Suzuki: Agreed. Counter with 17 months non-compete, limited to COO only. Non-solicit restricted to reports they personally hired. Leo Kumar: I’ll send the redline by 2025-05-27. They want to sign by 2025-06-01 to hit their fiscal year-end.
[phone buzzes on table]

Raj Petrov: Zara Petrov, I’ve gone through the latest batch of results from the protein folding experiments. The signal-to-noise ratio is worse than expected. Um, Zara Petrov: How much worse? We calibrated the setup in 2023-10-12. Raj Petrov: By a factor of 5x. The baseline drift is 22% over 12 hours. Nova Fischer: That explains why the replication at Caltech failed. They couldn’t reproduce our p-values. Raj Petrov: Exactly. I think the issue is environmental, not methodological. The humidity in our lab fluctuates between 46% and 79%. Zara Petrov: The grant deadline is 2025-08-17. If we can’t fix this in 2 weeks, we’ll have to submit preliminary data. Nova Fischer: Preliminary data won’t get us the $1.65M we need for the next phase. Raj Petrov: I have a proposal. Taylor Silva at MIT published a denoising protocol last month. It uses an auxiliary calibration standard. Honestly, Zara Petrov: I saw that. Science 2024. But it requires vacuum chamber retrofit. Raj Petrov: We already have the FPGA board from the old Project Nexus. I checked the inventory yesterday. Nova Fischer: That could work. What’s the timeline? Raj Petrov: 4 weeks for setup, 2 weeks for data collection. We’d have clean results by 2024-01-01. Zara Petrov: I’m concerned about authorship. If Raj Petrov implements this protocol, does Fatima Dubois get co-author credit? Raj Petrov: It’s a methods citation, not a collaboration. But to be safe, I’ll email them. Nova Fischer: Also, the IRB approval for the next human-subject phase expires on 2025-08-17. We need to renew. Zara Petrov: I’ll handle the IRB. Raj Petrov, you own the protocol migration. Nova Fischer, can you draft the preliminary paper so we have something ready? Nova Fischer: I can have an outline by 2025-11-10. The working title is "Novel approaches to protein folding under noisy conditions". Raj Petrov: Good. One more thing — the conference in Singapore is in 2025-10-18. If we get clean data, we should submit an abstract. Zara Petrov: Deadline for abstracts? Raj Petrov: 2025-01-30. 25 days from now.

Mei Popov: Hey Amara Okafor, I’ve been looking at the numbers for Project Ember. The current infrastructure costs are up to $254K per month, and we’re only serving 21K daily active users. Amara Okafor: Yeah, I saw the Grafana dashboard this morning. The Elixir + Phoenix + TimescaleDB stack is eating most of it. Radiant Energy can’t keep burning cash like this. Mei Popov: Exactly. On 2024-12-09, the CFO flagged it. She wants a 30% cut by Q3. Amara Okafor: Thirty percent? That’s aggressive. Um, What’s our runway if we don’t hit it? Mei Popov: About 10 months. But here’s the thing — I’ve been running experiments with Rust for the hot path. We could drop latency by 51% and cut compute by half. Amara Okafor: Rust? Last time we discussed this you were skeptical. What changed? Mei Popov: I spent a weekend rewriting the ingestion service. 2224 lines. The memory footprint went from 4GB to 180MB. Amara Okafor: Wow. Okay. But migration cost? Mei Popov: I estimated 5 engineer-months. The blocker is team skill gap — only 2 people know Rust. Um, Isolde White: I’m one of them. I can lead the migration if we get sign-off by 2025-03-27. Amara Okafor: Alright. Let’s draft a proposal. Well, I want three options: aggressive rewrite, conservative optimization, and status quo with cloud discounts. Mei Popov: I’ll have the doc ready by 2024-11-20. One concern though — the VP of Eng prefers Go for everything. He’ll push back. Amara Okafor: I know. I’ll handle him. Just make sure the numbers are bulletproof. Mei Popov: Also, there’s a security audit coming up on 2025-07-29. The pen-testers found 3 critical CVEs in our dependencies. Amara Okafor: Which ones? Mei Popov: Mostly in the legacy auth library. I’ve already patched 4 of them, but the rest require a breaking change to the JWT flow. Amara Okafor: So we’re talking migration + security + cost cut, all in the same quarter? Mei Popov: Unfortunately, yes. The audit findings have a hard remediation deadline of 2025-10-05. Amara Okafor: [sighs] Let’s bring this up at the all-hands. We need buy-in from product too, or we’ll be fighting on three fronts. Mei Popov: Agreed. By the way, Hugo Wilson from SRE mentioned they’re building an internal platform team. Might be worth aligning with them so we don’t duplicate effort. Look, Amara Okafor: Good call. I’ll reach out.
[shuffles papers]
[long pause]

Anyway, Amara Schneider: Okay, Skyler Singh. I’ve read draft 5 of "The Cartographer’s Lie". It’s 64502 words. Let’s dig in. Skyler Singh: I’m bracing myself. What’s the verdict? Amara Schneider: The prose is gorgeous. Your sentence-level craft is sharp. But the structure is fighting the story. Skyler Singh: Where? Amara Schneider: The midpoint happens at the 64% mark. That’s too late. Honestly, By then, the reader’s patience is thinning. Skyler Singh: I was worried about that. The inciting incident is strong — the fire in chapter 3 — but then I meander. Amara Schneider: Exactly. You have 5 subplots that don’t converge. The sibling rivalry thread just drops. Skyler Singh: I love that thread though. It reveals the protagonist’s cowardice. Amara Schneider: I know. But love isn’t enough. Either merge it with the A-plot or cut it. My instinct? Merge. Use the rivalry as the key to the climax. Skyler Singh: That could work. What about the antagonist? Beta readers say she’s unmotivated. Amara Schneider: She is. You tell us she’s cruel. But you never show us why she believes she’s righteous. Give her a lost child. Make her the hero of her own story. Skyler Singh: A POV chapter from her side? Amara Schneider: One. Just one. In the second act. 3359 words. It’ll reframe everything. Look, Skyler Singh: I’m worried about the genre. Is this cli-fi or literary fiction with cli-fi elements? Amara Schneider: That’s your central tension. Right now it’s neither fish nor fowl. Anyway, Agents will struggle to position it. Skyler Singh: So I need to commit. Lean into the cli-fi tropes or strip them out entirely. Amara Schneider: Yes. And honestly? Your voice is literary. The cli-fi framework is constraining you. Anyway, I’d pull back the plot machinery and let the character study breathe. Skyler Singh: That’s scary. The market wants high-concept. Amara Schneider: The market wants voice. Look at George Saunders. They break every rule and sell. Actually, Skyler Singh: Okay. I'll write a new outline by 2024-05-16. Cutting 29966 words. Amara Schneider: Don’t think of it as cutting. You’re excavating. The real book is under the rubble.
[long pause]

Zara Dubois: Sage Dubois, I’ve gone through the latest batch of results from the gravitational wave astronomy experiments. The signal-to-noise ratio is worse than expected. Sage Dubois: How much worse? We calibrated the setup in 2023-05-29. Zara Dubois: By a factor of 3x. The baseline drift is 39% over 12 hours. Avery Murphy: That explains why the replication at Imperial College failed. They couldn’t reproduce our p-values. Zara Dubois: Exactly. I think the issue is environmental, not methodological. The humidity in our lab fluctuates between 30% and 86%. Sage Dubois: The grant deadline is 2025-10-12. If we can’t fix this in 5 weeks, we’ll have to submit preliminary data. Avery Murphy: Preliminary data won’t get us the $553K we need for the next phase. Zara Dubois: I have a proposal. Skyler Suzuki at Oxford published a denoising protocol last month. It uses an auxiliary sensor. Sage Dubois: I saw that. Physical Review Letters 2024. But it requires cryogenic cooling. Zara Dubois: We already have the cooling unit from the old Project Prism. I checked the inventory yesterday. Avery Murphy: That could work. What’s the timeline? I mean, Zara Dubois: 3 weeks for setup, 3 weeks for data collection. We’d have clean results by 2025-04-25. I mean, Sage Dubois: I’m concerned about authorship. If Zara Dubois implements this protocol, does Drew Kowalski get co-author credit? Zara Dubois: It’s a methods citation, not a collaboration. But to be safe, I’ll email them. Avery Murphy: Also, the IRB approval for the next human-subject phase expires on 2025-10-12. We need to renew. Sage Dubois: I’ll handle the IRB. Zara Dubois, you own the protocol migration. Avery Murphy, can you draft the preliminary paper so we have something ready? Avery Murphy: I can have an outline by 2024-07-15. The working title is "Novel approaches to gravitational wave astronomy under noisy conditions". Zara Dubois: Good. One more thing — the conference in Vienna is in 2025-02-11. If we get clean data, we should submit an abstract. Sage Dubois: Deadline for abstracts? Zara Dubois: 2025-04-13. 25 days from now. [The team spends the next two hours whiteboarding the experimental redesign.]

Quinn Singh: Hey Lila Anderson, I’ve been looking at the numbers for Project Nexus. The current infrastructure costs are up to $1.04M per month, and we’re only serving 30K daily active users. Right, Lila Anderson: Yeah, I saw the Grafana dashboard this morning. The Java + Spring + Oracle stack is eating most of it. Cipher Law can’t keep burning cash like this. Quinn Singh: Exactly. On 2024-11-02, the CFO flagged it. She wants a 30% cut by Q3. Lila Anderson: Thirty percent? That’s aggressive. What’s our runway if we don’t hit it? Quinn Singh: About 9 months. But here’s the thing — I’ve been running experiments with Rust for the hot path. We could drop latency by 50% and cut compute by half. Lila Anderson: Rust? Last time we discussed this you were skeptical. What changed? Quinn Singh: I spent a weekend rewriting the ingestion service. 2351 lines. The memory footprint went from 4GB to 180MB. Lila Anderson: Wow. Okay. But migration cost? Quinn Singh: I estimated 2 engineer-months. The blocker is team skill gap — only 1 people know Rust. Right, Lila Anderson: Alright. Let’s draft a proposal. I want three options: aggressive rewrite, conservative optimization, and status quo with cloud discounts. Quinn Singh: I’ll have the doc ready by 2024-05-30. One concern though — the VP of Eng prefers Go for everything. He’ll push back. Lila Anderson: I know. I’ll handle him. Just make sure the numbers are bulletproof. Quinn Singh: Also, there’s a security audit coming up on 2025-03-31. The pen-testers found 8 critical CVEs in our dependencies. Lila Anderson: Which ones? Quinn Singh: Mostly in the legacy auth library. I’ve already patched 4 of them, but the rest require a breaking change to the JWT flow. Lila Anderson: So we’re talking migration + security + cost cut, all in the same quarter? Quinn Singh: Unfortunately, yes. The audit findings have a hard remediation deadline of 2025-06-22. Lila Anderson: [sighs] Let’s bring this up at the all-hands. We need buy-in from product too, or we’ll be fighting on three fronts. Quinn Singh: Agreed. By the way, Nadia Watanabe from SRE mentioned they’re building an internal platform team. Might be worth aligning with them so we don’t duplicate effort. Lila Anderson: Good call. I’ll reach out. [The meeting continues for another hour, diving into architecture diagrams.]

[leafs through notebook]
Quinn Singh: I’m worried about vendor lock-in. What if we need to migrate in year two? Quinn Singh: Let’s not forget the edge deployment. 7 regions need local caching. Lila Anderson: We should consider sharding by tenant_id. The current monolithic approach won’t scale past 50K users. Quinn Singh: Did you see the post-mortem from 2024-07-10? The root cause was a stale DNS cache. Quinn Singh: I ran a load test at 5960 RPS. P99 spiked to 324ms. Honestly, Quinn Singh: Did you see the post-mortem from 2024-12-20? The root cause was a stale DNS cache. Lila Anderson: We should consider sharding by tenant_id. The current monolithic approach won’t scale past 82K users. Lila Anderson: I spoke with Nova Tanaka at the vendor. Look, They’re offering a 22% discount if we commit to 3 years. You know, Lila Anderson: I’ve been prototyping with Envoy. The config is verbose but the performance is solid. Quinn Singh: The Redis cluster had 3 failover events last week. I think we need Sentinel + persistence. Lila Anderson: Let’s wrap. Action items: proposal by 2024-05-30, security patches by 2026-10-30, vendor call by 2025-10-04. Quinn Singh: Got it. I’ll sync the doc in Slack.

Sven Jensen: Okay, Kofi O'Brien. I’ve read draft 5 of "Under the Amber Moon". It’s 119859 words. Let’s dig in. Kofi O'Brien: I’m bracing myself. What’s the verdict? Sven Jensen: The prose is gorgeous. Your sentence-level craft is exceptional. But the structure is fighting the story. Kofi O'Brien: Where? Sven Jensen: The midpoint happens at the 61% mark. That’s too late. Right, By then, the reader’s patience is thinning. Kofi O'Brien: I was worried about that. The inciting incident is strong — the fire in chapter 2 — but then I meander. Sven Jensen: Exactly. You have 6 subplots that don’t converge. The estranged father thread just drops. Kofi O'Brien: I love that thread though. It reveals the protagonist’s obsession. Sven Jensen: I know. But love isn’t enough. Either merge it with the A-plot or cut it. My instinct? I mean, Merge. Use the rivalry as the key to the climax. Kofi O'Brien: That could work. What about the antagonist? Actually, Beta readers say she’s unmotivated. Sven Jensen: She is. You tell us she’s cruel. But you never show us why she believes she’s righteous. Give her a betrayal. Make her the hero of her own story. Kofi O'Brien: A POV chapter from her side? Sven Jensen: One. Just one. In the second act. 4533 words. It’ll reframe everything. Kofi O'Brien: I’m worried about the genre. Is this cli-fi or literary fiction with cli-fi elements? Sven Jensen: That’s your central tension. Right now it’s neither fish nor fowl. Agents will struggle to position it. Kofi O'Brien: So I need to commit. Lean into the cli-fi tropes or strip them out entirely. Sven Jensen: Yes. And honestly? Your voice is literary. The cli-fi framework is constraining you. I’d pull back the plot machinery and let the character study breathe. Kofi O'Brien: That’s scary. The market wants high-concept. Sven Jensen: The market wants voice. Look at Ottessa Moshfegh. They break every rule and sell. Kofi O'Brien: Okay. I'll write a new outline by 2023-06-08. Cutting 27521 words. Sven Jensen: Don’t think of it as cutting. You’re excavating. The real book is under the rubble. [They spend the next hour workshopping a pivotal scene line by line.]

Kofi O'Brien: I want the reader to work for it. Kofi O'Brien: I was trying to create atmosphere. Sven Jensen: That can work if the actions are legible. If she burns the letter, the reader needs to know why without being told. Well, Sven Jensen: That can work if the actions are legible. If she burns the letter, the reader needs to know why without being told. Kofi O'Brien: I got feedback from Mei Miller at a workshop. They said the ending was "earned but predictable."

Kofi O'Brien: What if the protagonist never explains her motivation? Just acts? Sven Jensen: New outline. Agents smell desperation. If you pitch a book you’re still fixing, they’ll pass. Sven Jensen: This paragraph has 7 adjectives in 4 sentences. Actually, Pick one. Sven Jensen: Workshop feedback is a compass, not a map. Trust your own sense of the ending. Kofi O'Brien: I got feedback from Alex Chen at a workshop. They said the ending was "earned but predictable."

[long pause]
Kofi O'Brien: What if the protagonist never explains her motivation? Just acts? Sven Jensen: Final note: read Gilead. Notice how silence operates. That’s what you’re missing. Kofi O'Brien: I’ll pick it up today. Next session? Sven Jensen: 2024-03-08. Bring the revised chapter 3 and the antagonist POV.

Priya Ortiz: Kenji Watanabe, I’ve gone through the latest batch of results from the causal inference experiments. The signal-to-noise ratio is worse than expected. So, Kenji Watanabe: How much worse? We calibrated the setup in 2024-07-17. Priya Ortiz: By a factor of 6x. The baseline drift is 31% over 5 hours. Marcus Ali: That explains why the replication at MIT failed. They couldn’t reproduce our p-values. Priya Ortiz: Exactly. I think the issue is environmental, not methodological. The humidity in our lab fluctuates between 42% and 67%. Kenji Watanabe: The grant deadline is 2025-07-16. If we can’t fix this in 3 weeks, we’ll have to submit preliminary data. Marcus Ali: Preliminary data won’t get us the $1.72M we need for the next phase. Priya Ortiz: I have a proposal. Alex Kim at Oxford published a denoising protocol last month. It uses an auxiliary sensor. Kenji Watanabe: I saw that. Science 2023. But it requires custom FPGA programming. Priya Ortiz: We already have the FPGA board from the old Project Zenith. I checked the inventory yesterday. Marcus Ali: That could work. What’s the timeline? Priya Ortiz: 2 weeks for setup, 3 weeks for data collection. We’d have clean results by 2024-07-27. Kenji Watanabe: I’m concerned about authorship. If Priya Ortiz implements this protocol, does Elara Andersson get co-author credit? Priya Ortiz: It’s a methods citation, not a collaboration. But to be safe, I’ll email them. Marcus Ali: Also, the IRB approval for the next human-subject phase expires on 2025-07-16. We need to renew. Kenji Watanabe: I’ll handle the IRB. Priya Ortiz, you own the protocol migration. Marcus Ali, can you draft the preliminary paper so we have something ready? Marcus Ali: I can have an outline by 2024-03-21. The working title is "Novel approaches to causal inference under noisy conditions". Priya Ortiz: Good. One more thing — the conference in Boston is in 2025-02-21. If we get clean data, we should submit an abstract. Kenji Watanabe: Deadline for abstracts? Priya Ortiz: 2025-12-03. 14 days from now. [The team spends the next two hours whiteboarding the experimental redesign.]

[typing sounds]
Marcus Ali: We can draft a DUA. Uh, Ravi Bakshi in our legal office does them fast. Marcus Ali: We can draft a DUA. Lila Jensen in our legal office does them fast. Kenji Watanabe: We need more power. Sample size of 72 is too small for this effect size. Priya Ortiz: Budget allows for 223 subjects if we cut the imaging budget. Priya Ortiz: Still significant at p<0.05? Priya Ortiz: The standard error in the control group is 10% higher than treated. Anyway, Marcus Ali: I ran a blinded re-analysis. The effect size drops from Cohen’s d=0.45 to 0.55. Marcus Ali: I’d rather keep imaging and find a cheaper recruitment channel. Kenji Watanabe: Have we ruled out observer bias? 1 of the grad students knew which group was which. Priya Ortiz: That’s a collaboration, which complicates data ownership. Kenji Watanabe: Let’s adjourn. Action items assigned. Next lab meeting: 2025-10-29.

Anyway, But love isn’t enough. Either merge it with the A-plot or cut it. My instinct? Merge. I mean, Use the rivalry as the key to the climax. Raj Murphy: That could work. What about the antagonist? Raj Murphy: A POV chapter from her side? I mean, Bruno Dubois: One. Just one. In the second act. 3430 words. It’ll reframe everything. Raj Murphy: I’m worried about the genre. Bruno Dubois: The market wants voice. Look at Carmen Maria Machado. They break every rule and sell. Raj Murphy: Okay. I'll write a new outline by 2023-06-10. Cutting 17855 words. Amara Rossi: Morning team. New consult, 47-year-old with Parkinson's disease. I mean, Referred from Johns Hopkins on 2023-02-25. Right, Sam Schneider: What’s the presentation? Amara Rossi: Progressive symptoms over 7 months. Baseline labs show elevated LDH. Imaging shows lymphadenopathy. Taylor Lindqvist: Has adalimumab been tried? Amara Rossi: Yes, 6 cycles. Partial response, but the patient developed infusion reactions. Sam Schneider: Grade? Amara Rossi: Grade 3. Dose reduced by 50%. Taylor Lindqvist: I saw a case series last month — 28 patients with refractory Parkinson's disease responded to allogeneic transplant. Sam Schneider: What were the inclusion criteria? Taylor Lindqvist: ECOG 1, no prior autoimmune disease. This patient qualifies on 2 of 3 criteria. Amara Rossi: The family wants aggressive treatment. But the patient’s comorbidities — Type 2 diabetes — make high-intensity therapy risky. Um, Sam Schneider: What does palliative care think? Amara Rossi: They’re consulting tomorrow. My gut says offer the radioligand trial but set clear stop criteria. Taylor Lindqvist: There’s a compassionate use program for a senolytic combination. Enrollment closes 2025-07-17. Sam Schneider: Is the mechanism aligned with this patient’s biomarker profile? Taylor Lindqvist: The patient is HER2 low. The trial requires exactly that. Amara Rossi: That changes things. But compassionate use means no randomization. Actually, We lose the safety net. Sam Schneider: The patient is refractory to standard of care. At this stage, the risk-benefit shifts. Taylor Lindqvist: I agree. We should present both options — trial versus best supportive care — and let the patient decide. Amara Rossi: I’ll schedule the family conference for 2025-02-24. Sam Schneider, can you pull the survival data from 4 comparable cases? Look, Sam Schneider: I have 11 cases in our registry. Median survival after adalimumab failure is 11 months. [The team reviews imaging and pathology slides for the next hour.]
[clears throat]
[pauses, looks out window]
[clears throat]

I told them we’d optimize for symptom control regardless of trial enrollment. Amara Rossi: The PET-CT shows SUV max of 12 in the bone marrow. Taylor Dubois: $30K cap, or 31% of purchase price, whichever is lower. Orion White: Our board will accept a $97K cap, but not uncapped. What’s your read on their flexibility? Expected liability exposure is $3K. Even a $165K cap has 81% coverage probability. Orion White: Good data. Use it in the counter. Second issue? Taylor Dubois: Earn-out structure. They want 24% of value tied to EBITDA targets over 2 years. Orion White: That’s standard. What’s the objection? Taylor Dubois: The targets are 30% above our internal projections. If we miss year one, the clawback is punitive. Alex Novak: I modeled three scenarios: base case, bear case, stress case. In stress case, we lose 46% of the earn-out. Orion White: Can we negotiate a sliding scale instead of cliff vesting? Taylor Dubois: That’s my recommendation. Look, Pro-rata for every 15% of target achieved. Orion White: Draft that language. Third issue? Taylor Dubois: Restrictive covenants. They want a 27-month non-compete for the founding team. Standard in this jurisdiction is 10 months. Alex Novak: And the non-solicit covers all employees, not just direct reports. That’s overbroad. Orion White: Agreed. Counter with 12 months non-compete, limited to CTO only. Non-solicit restricted to reports they personally hired. Taylor Dubois: I’ll send the redline by 2025-09-15. They want to sign by 2025-05-04 to hit their fiscal year-end. Sofia Williams: Thanks for joining, Quinn Tanaka. Uh, We’re here to finalize the Pinnacle Data–Zenith Cloud acquisition term sheet. Deal value is $6.68M. Quinn Tanaka: I’ve reviewed the draft from 2024-08-19. There are 11 open issues, but three are material. Sofia Williams: Walk me through them. Quinn Tanaka: First, the right of first refusal. Pinnacle Data wants uncapped liability for tax indemnity. That’s a non-starter. Sofia Williams: What’s their opening position? Look, Quinn Tanaka: $12K cap, or 10% of purchase price, whichever is lower. Sofia Williams: Our board will accept a $91K cap, but not uncapped. What’s your read on their flexibility? Quinn Tanaka: Their outside counsel — Casey Popov — signaled movement on a $90K cap during the dinner last week. But their CFO is pushing back. Skyler Kowalski: I ran the risk model. Honestly, Expected liability exposure is $16K. Even a $199K cap has 95% coverage probability. Sofia Williams: Good data. Use it in the counter. Second issue? Quinn Tanaka: Earn-out structure. They want 27% of value tied to EBITDA targets over 4 years. Sofia Williams: That’s standard. What’s the objection? Quinn Tanaka: The targets are 22% above our internal projections. If we miss year one, the clawback is punitive. Skyler Kowalski: I modeled three scenarios: base case, bear case, stress case. In stress case, we lose 34% of the earn-out. Look, Sofia Williams: Can we negotiate a sliding scale instead of cliff vesting? Quinn Tanaka: That’s my recommendation. Pro-rata for every 11% of target achieved. Sofia Williams: Draft that language. Third issue? Quinn Tanaka: Restrictive covenants. They want a 21-month non-compete for the founding team. Standard in this jurisdiction is 8 months. Skyler Kowalski: And the non-solicit covers all employees, not just direct reports. That’s overbroad. Sofia Williams: Agreed. Counter with 13 months non-compete, limited to CTO only. Non-solicit restricted to reports they personally hired. Quinn Tanaka: I’ll send the redline by 2024-08-15. Look, They want to sign by 2025-07-15 to hit their fiscal year-end. [The negotiation extends into the evening, with calls to both principals.]
[sips coffee]
[door opens briefly]

Sofia Williams: What about the reps and warranties insurance? Quinn Tanaka: They’ll want the fee to survive. Let’s offer 1% in that scenario. Sofia Williams: Zenith Cloud’s counsel just called. They’re insisting on a hell-or-high-water clause. Sofia Williams: What about the reps and warranties insurance? Quinn Tanaka: Escrow is currently 15% of purchase price. We could bump to 17% for 17 months. Quinn Tanaka: That’s clever. It softens the blow. Sofia Williams: Then we offer a seller note for the incremental escrow. 7% interest, 13 month term. Quinn Tanaka: A 4% breakup fee is market. Four percent is aggressive. Sofia Williams: Propose 2025-08-21. If no approval by then, either party walks with no breakup fee. Sofia Williams: Can we push some of that back to the seller through escrow? Skyler Kowalski: The insurer excluded environmental. We’d retain that risk. Sofia Williams: Then we offer a seller note for the incremental escrow. 5% interest, 15 month term. Sofia Williams: Alright. Send the revised terms tonight. I want a principled response by 2024-08-15. If they’re close, we fly to Dubai for close.

Fatima Tanaka: Morning team. New consult, 75-year-old with narcolepsy with cataplexy. Referred from UCSF Medical Center on 2024-01-23. Ravi Chen: What’s the presentation? Fatima Tanaka: Progressive symptoms over 6 months. Baseline labs show abnormal LFTs. Imaging shows ground-glass opacities. Bruno Nakamura: Has semaglutide been tried? Fatima Tanaka: Yes, 2 cycles. Partial response, but the patient developed infusion reactions. Ravi Chen: Grade? Fatima Tanaka: Grade 2. Look, Dose reduced by 36%. Bruno Nakamura: I saw a case series last month — 9 patients with refractory narcolepsy with cataplexy responded to allogeneic transplant. Ravi Chen: What were the inclusion criteria? Bruno Nakamura: ECOG 1, no prior CNS involvement. This patient qualifies on 2 of 4 criteria. Fatima Tanaka: The family wants aggressive treatment. But the patient’s comorbidities — atrial fibrillation — make high-intensity therapy risky. Well, Ravi Chen: What does palliative care think? Fatima Tanaka: They’re consulting tomorrow. My gut says offer the CAR-T trial but set clear stop criteria. Bruno Nakamura: There’s a compassionate use program for a senolytic combination. Enrollment closes 2025-09-03. Ravi Chen: Is the mechanism aligned with this patient’s biomarker profile? Bruno Nakamura: The patient is HER2 low. The trial requires exactly that. Fatima Tanaka: That changes things. But compassionate use means no randomization. We lose the safety net. Ravi Chen: The patient is refractory to standard of care. At this stage, the risk-benefit shifts. Bruno Nakamura: I agree. We should present both options — trial versus best supportive care — and let the patient decide. Fatima Tanaka: I’ll schedule the family conference for 2024-04-15. Ravi Chen, can you pull the survival data from 5 comparable cases? Right, Ravi Chen: I have 9 cases in our registry. Median survival after semaglutide failure is 10 months. [The team reviews imaging and pathology slides for the next hour.]

[phone buzzes on table]
Fatima Tanaka: The family asked about quality of life. I told them we’d optimize for symptom control regardless of trial enrollment. Fatima Tanaka: Pathology from the 2024-09-28 biopsy showed 88% necrosis. Ravi Chen: Good. Trust is fragile here. Fatima Tanaka: Pathology from the 2024-06-24 biopsy showed 62% granulomatous reaction. Ravi Chen: I’d like a second opinion from Ingrid Okafor at UCSF Medical Center. They specialize in narcolepsy with cataplexy. Fatima Tanaka: Pathology from the 2024-05-08 biopsy showed 74% granulomatous reaction. Ravi Chen: I’d like a second opinion from Fatima Okafor at UCSF Medical Center. They specialize in narcolepsy with cataplexy. Ravi Chen: I’d like a second opinion from Taylor Cohen at UCSF Medical Center. They specialize in narcolepsy with cataplexy. Fatima Tanaka: Pathology from the 2024-03-03 biopsy showed 75% fibrosis. Bruno Nakamura: Patchy areas of central necrosis in 4 of 7 lesions. Ravi Chen: I’d like a second opinion from Viktor Silva at UCSF Medical Center. They specialize in narcolepsy with cataplexy. Actually, Fatima Tanaka: The PET-CT shows SUV max of 8 in the liver. Ravi Chen: That’s a red flag. Well, If it hits 4 before the trial starts, they’re ineligible. Ravi Chen: I’d like a second opinion from Kofi Rossi at UCSF Medical Center. They specialize in narcolepsy with cataplexy. Ravi Chen: That’s a red flag. If it hits 4 before the trial starts, they’re ineligible. Well, Fatima Tanaka: The patient’s ECOG declined from 0 to 3 in the past 3 weeks. Fatima Tanaka: Decision: proceed with compassionate use application. Ravi Chen, handle the informed consent. Bruno Nakamura, manage the bridge therapy. Round again in 37 hours.

Ravi Dubois: Okay team, we need to talk about the trajectory. Our Series A was $450K in 2024-07-10, and we’re burning $144K a month. Theo Bakshi: The runway math is brutal. Ravi Dubois: I interviewed 23 enterprise customers. 72% said they’d pay 1233 per seat annually if we had SSO and audit logs. Elara Jackson: Enterprise features? Our whole brand is simplicity. Won’t that alienate our core users? Ravi Dubois: It might. But our core users convert at 4% to paid. Enterprise would convert at 31%. The math is clear. Honestly, Theo Bakshi: What about the team? We’re 10 people. Enterprise sales requires a completely different muscle. Right, Ravi Dubois: I know. So, I’ve been talking to Talia Wilson, who scaled Meridian Bio from zero to $21.23M ARR. She’s open to advising. Elara Jackson: Advising isn’t hiring. We’d need at least 2 experienced AEs and a customer success function. Ravi Dubois: Correct. I’ve modeled it. The pivot adds $128K in monthly burn for the first 6 months, then turns profitable by 2025-12-02. Theo Bakshi: I’m not opposed, but I want to test it. Can we run a closed beta with 7 design partners? Ravi Dubois: That’s the plan. I’ve already soft-committed with Diego Johnson at Yield Finance and Oscar Popov at Flux Analytics. Elara Jackson: What about the board? Oscar Thomas from the lead investor is pretty conservative. Ravi Dubois: I’m presenting on 2025-12-23. I need you both aligned before then. If we go in divided, the board will hesitate. Theo Bakshi: I’ll support it if we have a rollback plan. If the beta doesn’t hit 8 paying customers by 2025-08-19, we revert. Elara Jackson: Same here. Also, I want to keep the freemium tier. It’s our top-of-funnel. Ravi Dubois: Absolutely. The pivot is additive, not replacement. [The whiteboard fills with projections and timelines.]

Last time we discussed this you were skeptical. What changed? Skyler Garcia: I spent a weekend rewriting the ingestion service. 1509 lines. The memory footprint went from 4GB to 180MB. Reese Jones: Wow. Okay. But migration cost? Skyler Garcia: I estimated 5 engineer-months. The blocker is team skill gap — only 2 people know Rust. Alex Novak: I’m one of them. I can lead the migration if we get sign-off by 2025-05-06. Reese Jones: Alright. Let’s draft a proposal. Skyler Garcia: Mostly in the legacy auth library. I’ve already patched 2 of them, but the rest require a breaking change to the JWT flow. Reese Jones: So we’re talking migration + security + cost cut, all in the same quarter? Skyler Garcia: Unfortunately, yes. The audit findings have a hard remediation deadline of 2025-06-06.
